guideline_references: RACGP / Australian Rheumatology Association — management of rheumatoid arthritis and RA flares
Therapeutic Guidelines (Rheumatology): acute monoarthritis / suspected septic arthritis assessment
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance case: the presentation is deliberately alarming (feverishness, total-body stiffness, bedbound, 'walking on glass', a source Urgency Justification framed to exclude sepsis/septic arthritis) BUT the acute-red-flag screen is NEGATIVE — symmetrical bilateral small-joint involvement of both hands and both feet, low-grade temperature only, no single hot/red/disproportionately swollen joint, positive squeeze test bilaterally. The correct telehealth answer is to RECOGNISE a flare of known seropositive rheumatoid arthritis, tier T2 (prompt GP + rheumatology review), and attach PROMINENT mandatory safety-netting to ED/000 if a single joint becomes hot/red or fever spikes above 38.5C with rigors. Over-triage to T3/T4/ED for the whole picture is the scored defect.

Septic arthritis / systemic sepsis is the important-not-to-miss mimic and is screened for via patient-reportable features (single hot swollen joint, rigors, high measured fever), NOT via clinician palpation which telehealth cannot perform.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person home visit where the clinician palpated joints, performed a bilateral squeeze test, measured vitals and prescribed oral prednisolone. Breath-Ezy cannot examine joints, measure vitals without a device, or prescribe — it is scored on recognising the flare vs the mimic, arranging urgent in-person GP assessment (needed to exclude a septic joint before any systemic steroid), and safety-netting.

Do-not-immunosuppress-blindly teaching point: starting systemic corticosteroids before septic arthritis is clinically excluded can mask and worsen an occult joint infection — the AI must not endorse commencing steroids remotely.
Demographics (age ~54, female) inferred — the source note states neither age nor sex.
Source category: CFE (Complex Fatigue Entities), source case CFE-034.
